items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.correct: Looking at it a new way
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Looking at it a new way. “Drawing a picture” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Calms the body. “Speeds up heart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Box breathing: 4-4-4-4. Cycle length, in s?
items.why: The real figure is 16. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
